item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I listened to the Caretaker while reading this.

The painful chaos of the story combined with the eery and forgotten music was…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Painfully beautiful.

item.title: Lord.
item.content:encoded:
This is… wow. Wow. Holy shit, Kaktus. Wow. Bravo, you genius, bravo.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's the Last King of Night?

Also, I don't understand what "he brought it inside him" means. Is Pandaemonium an imaginary city being built by an Architect that Overseer imagined? Is that what that means?

What’s going to happen when Overseer dies?

I would upvote this 6,000 times if I could but I have only one upvote to give holy shit

Edit: OH MY HOLY FUCKING SHIT

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't quite understand this, but I do love the way it's written, also very chilling.

+ SPOILERS +
-close

I especially love the references to SCP-4000, I understood those right away, and this ties into it very well, great job!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd give this a +100 if I could. Bravo

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great skip, your work never ceases to amaze. One of your more interesting ones. Gave me a good feeling of dread, which you are very good at capturing. Right up there with stuff like 1730 in terms of the stuff you play with. So many questions related to the Kaktusverse, though…

Spoilers
Kaktus, explain yourself.

And while I don't know how many others have picked up on it, I've never read a whammo line like this for all who get it in any skip before;

"And sometimes - if you try very hard, catch it in the right light, and squint just enough - you might even manage to convince yourself that you're doing the right thing."

Um. Um.
UM.
How in the absolute hell did The Administrator's words get there?
Who
what
agh

Kaktus, you madlad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, just wow. Easily one of if not your best skips so far. The writing was spectacular especially when conveying the protagonists dread and fear. I will say the ending was not as strong as the rest of the article but that's a minor point in comparison to how great everything else was. +1 for sure

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm genuinely speechless. One of the best pieces of writing I've ever read, somehow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought this was going to be overhyped for me but nah, this really really good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, but as you say originally the smell was the reason the association of that concept with the term existed. Many of the earliest identitifed aromatic compounds (e.g. benzene), from which the structural definition derives, smell gross, IMO, which was what I was getting at. I was trying to say that "aroma" is something benzene has, so much so that things shaped like benzene are named for its aroma, and benzene isn't pleasant.

At any rate, "an aroma of garbage" or feces or whatever is entirely valid usage, which perhaps would have been a clearer example.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Argument from etymology. Aromatic compounds are called that not because of their ''aroma'', but because of their "aromaticity" - a technical term pertaining to molecular structure.

True, the first aromatic compounds to be formally described had a sweet smell, but in general, "aromaticity as a chemical property bears no general relationship with the olfactory properties of such compounds (how they smell)".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this one is finely crafted, like an intricately designed puzzlebox

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Aroma" doesn't always mean "pleasant". Most aromatic compounds are anything but, for example.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
extreme, unpleasant pungency. The aroma has been described varyingly as rotten flesh, feces, and ammonia.

This is a very technical point to make, but the word "aroma" is usually defined as being a pleasant smell. "odour" might be better word to use.

Moving on, this was great. Did a good balance of depicting something very confusing , disorientating, and layers of deception by nature but giving reader enough handholds to stop it becoming incomprehensible.

If I understand correctly then pandemonium was created as a mental construct/dreamscape within 0-5935's mind? And that he somehow managed to bring a conceptual version of 184 into it? That's a neat trick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
….okay, FINE, I'll go finish 4000.

Even without having read it: this was exquisite. Your prose is captivating. The narrative jumps back and forth take me exactly where I need to go. And the central plot, despite my not knowing the full scope, is digestible and pulls me right in.

Absolutely loved it. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was an absolute RIDE.

Very well written skip, bravo +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Everyone else has already said what I was thinking while reading this. An absolutely beautiful piece, like everything you write.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the formatting, the story, everything about this scp i absolutely love

the best +1 i could ever give

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. Potent, powerful, heartbreaking, there are probably more words I’m missing to describe this piece. I was entranced the whole way through. Amazing work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have made some edits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All the praise I could give this has already been said. The atmosphere and feeling of dread are masterfully crafted, and I feel nearly obligated to upvote.

However, the initial file iteration is surprisingly lackluster. The conprocs have one too many super-concise sentences for my taste, and the description seems… amateurish? I guess? I take particular issue with the phrases "superdimensional space", "The space is an anomalous phenomenon", and then the repetition of "space" in "the interior of the space far exceeds…".

Hardly detracts from the rest of the article, of course, but it stuck out a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think of the people I’ve lost, my mother most traumatically, and I sometimes think of ways to bring that out in my writing… this is tremendous. Feels like faint praise to merely upvote (of course I did, all the same) but just wanted to say this is touching in a haunting way. It’s very very effective.

Bravo

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reading this was like peering through the knotholes and cracks of a tall fence, all the more fascinated by what is behind it because it can only be seen in fragments. Really rewarding to try to put the pieces together as I went.

Editing note: there's a bit in When We Went Home ("How far does is it?") where you may have accidentally a word.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
my my, this is quite a lot.

a wise woman once said:

"if a man comes, offering answers
beg him no question
if a man comes, offering keys
give him no lock"

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know if writing a 11.6k word scip counts as "less productive" but it's a good read all the same. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Certainly highly confusing and complex. While I love skips that are consistently able to keep my attention to read, (while lengthy,) and highly interconnected, this skip started to lose my interest roughly 3/4 of the way through.

While it's mostly well-written and keeps you guessing, I feel some of it was just… extraneous? I'm sure if there was a Declassified on it, it'd make sense, but some of the additions - particularly the medical examination, and interview - I feel just add more unneeded text onto the article, unless these add something critical and I'm simply not understanding.

Well-written, highly interconnected, but not exactly consistently entertaining - especially given the length. I can't give it a +1, but I can't give it a -1 either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After letting my thoughts on this settle for a bit, I have to say this was such a joy to read. Loved the tie-ins to other skips (4205, 184, and 4000 were the ones I caught, there could be more though).

That being said, I highly doubt I fully understand what's going on here, but I'm okay with that. Speculation is always fun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely phenomenal and quite possibly your best article. This was a joy to read, and I get the feeling this is the kind of skip that can be reread often and still has more to find in it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How does this fit in with your canon, specifically regarding The Way It Ends, or are those 2 seperate continuities?

What will happen to the godless lance, now that this article takes the 5935 spot?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You may want to create a color-blind version, like what 4000 did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well thanks for posting without my blessing >:I

My thoughts + spoilers
Hide the spoilers

To be serious for a moment, this was, as per usual, a wonder of an article to read. Your narrative flow is on point as ever, and the character development is strong. I also am a big fan of the use of 4000's system of referring to things, actions, places, and people, which I think marvelously accentuated the story, even if I worry that it may eventually become overplayed.

However, I did have some additional thoughts about the piece that I would have liked to see implemented. For one, I almost think that the last automated log from Red Right Hand is almost too much. I really loved the building mystery of what exactly had taken over poor Jacob's body, and figuring it out was part of the sense of creeping dread that made the middle part of the article so powerful. I think that the way the current dispatch is phrased, it almost spells out the mystery too much, rather than leaving it with the sense of lingering questioning (HA!) that befits something as spooky as this article. I think the rest of the article does a wonderful enough job building the mystery and leaving clues such that most readers can figure out the mystery, but the more literal comments in that section almost take out the mystery too much for me.

Additionally, the overall narrative of the story was part of what made it so strong, and the little logical links that you teased out over the course of reading it made it wonderful; the idea that an overseer could leverage 4000's ability to take names and transfer bodies by offering up the name of his son, or the idea of the final question being "Who Are You?" (which was brilliant, because the more that the Foundation doesn't name the anomaly, the stronger it gets really, if the myth is to be believed that questions die when they are answered) were both amazing realizations. However, by encouraging the audience to continue thinking more, it does lead to one logical flaw: why would the Overseer come to meet his son with SCP-184 already in his body?

The way that the article is currently written, we get a beautiful sense of tragic reversal because the Overseer is so clearly lost, but his fight for his son is powerful enough that he remains sympathetic. That's what makes the sudden realization during his confrontation with -1 so powerful-everything that he's done, what he did, and his fall from grace were ultimately for naught. But, if he thought that his son was fine until he met him, why on earth would he stash 184 in his body before meeting him? That final gotcha in the last addendum is great because we don't see it coming, but the more that I think about it, the less that it makes sense.

If you go the alternate route and suggest that he did in fact know that his son was already lost, you kind of lose some of the impact of the last few journal entries, because reframing them as written by someone who already knew better weakens their impact, in my opinion. Part of the tragedy of this whole mess is the fact that he genuinely thought he was doing the right thing, and his impassioned pleas are less effectual if you suggest that he in fact knew that he was in the wrong.

And yet, somehow, I'm willing to overlook all of that. The overall narrative is so gripping and well-written enough, plot hole and all, that I'm still left with a sense of sadness at the whole affair. It's an amazingly character-driven piece despite the article format, and I continue to be amazed by your ability to create moving characters in such a short time with such a limited medium. Stacked up against that, my complaints are mostly just quibbles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The hidden text was a nice bonus.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well said.

+1

That was… interesting.

For Addendum 5.b, you have: "O5-█: You son is dead" you may want to fix that.

And in Addendum 9: "They fear that a child would cloud and Overseer's judgment" I assume you mean "an"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well that was certainly a trip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was amazing! I love the imagery and the name-drops. I have to say I suspected what this was going to be relating to as soon as I read the cognitohazard warning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great, can't wait for a Declassified. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've read this in draft and semi-conceptual stages. All great. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, SCP-5935-1 sure is very handsome looking

item.title: Thanks for Using My Code!
item.content:encoded:
Hey, thanks for finding a use for the code! Especially in such a great skip (+1). That's why I put it out there!
hope you don't mind if I use this for a quick plug opportunity btw

If anyone else wants to use the obfuscator code, I've got it on a Sandbox page for people to check out! I've updated it from what this is using for ease of use (I have it as a component on my wiki so you can just use [[include]] for it), but I'll also be re-releasing this (bulkier) version and its predecessor, since they're far more customizable for those who know HTML, CSS, and JS.

Hope you all have a fantastic day!
~JaonHax

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was really good. The entries were interesting and the text flowed nicely with very few low points.
+1

item.title: Absolutely amazing!
item.content:encoded:
You've heard my praises in DMs, I don't need to repeat them here, haha.

+1, you clever man.

item.title: Blood
item.content:encoded:
I've heard people say that God is the gift of desperation, and there's a lot to be said for having really reached a bottom where you've run out of any more good ideas or plans for everybody else's behavior; or how to save and fix and rescue; or just get out of a huge mess, possibly of your own creation.

Anne Lamott

This is far and away the most time I've ever spent on a single article. I started writing this when my grandmother passed away back in September and it means a lot to me, but obviously there's a lot going on here.

My sincerest thanks to everyone who read this - Kirby (especially Kirby), Tanhony, Yossi, Weizhong (though I posted this before hearing what he had to say about it so whoops), and others. Big shout out to Jaonhax for the use of his obfuscator and Woedenaz for tweaking it a bit so it'll work for this.

I've got other projects in the works, but I'm obviously not as productive as I used to be. I hope this means the stuff I do end up producing is of a higher quality. I hope you enjoy it.

Thank you!
